news.source: ABC News & Headlines – Australian Broadcasting Corporation

news.title: Australia 31-33 Ireland: Nations Championship rugby union international – as it happened

sections.heading: UK residency strategy prioritises long-term financial survival over home advantage
sections.body: Fiji's choice to play home fixtures in the UK highlights a strategic push for financial sustainability. Facing a strict 25,000-capacity tournament minimum, the union opted for European gates. Fiji Rugby chairman John Sanday explained: "It’s a transformational opportunity for us. Never before would we have been able to have this kind of revenues, which we then can invest back into our facilities and high performance."

sections.heading: Recovery focus dominates preparations with no new squad setbacks reported
sections.body: No fresh squad injuries were reported in today’s coverage following Saturday’s bruising 8-73 defeat to England. The coaching staff is focusing heavily on physical recovery and defensive alignment sessions after conceding 112 points across their opening two fixtures. With a demanding travel schedule between Cardiff, Liverpool, and Edinburgh, managing squad fatigue is the primary concern before naming the matchday 23 later this week.

sections.heading: Scotland deal with front-five injuries after bruising Springboks loss
sections.body: Gregor Townsend's squad return to Murrayfield searching for answers after their 42-28 loss to South Africa. The hosts face immediate selection issues, with a Glasgow lock ruled out for weeks and requiring surgery, while their vice-captain undergoes assessment following a failed head injury assessment. Despite these setbacks, Scotland represent a formidable threat, with two-thirds of tickets already sold for the Edinburgh clash.

sections.heading: Fiji seek historic Murrayfield response to escape foot of the table
sections.body: Fiji goes into the weekend sitting 12th of 12 on the log, desperately seeking to arrest a difficult start to the tournament. Having suffered heavy defeats to Wales and England, the team must secure a positive result against Scotland to begin climbing the standings. Another loss would leave them isolated at the bottom, making a strong performance crucial for both pride and critical tournament revenue.

sections.heading: Quirke called up to replace Mitchell
sections.body: Scrum-half Raffi Quirke has been drafted into the England squad ahead of Saturday's Nations Championship match against Argentina. Quirke joins the group as an injury replacement following the loss of Alex Mitchell. With the squad preparing for the trip to South America, the half-back has a major opportunity to stake his claim under head coach Steve Borthwick.

sections.heading: Mitchell ruled out with hamstring injury
sections.body: Scrum-half Alex Mitchell has been ruled out of Saturday's Test against Argentina after sustaining a hamstring injury. The blow, picked up during the weekend's action, is a significant setback for England's tactical plans. Mitchell's absence from the final Nations Championship fixture forces a reshuffle in the half-back department as the medical team begins his rehabilitation process.

sections.heading: Los Pumas pose formidable home test
sections.body: Argentina represent a formidable challenge on home soil. While England won the last meeting between the sides with a 27-23 victory in November, touring South America always presents a hostile environment. Los Pumas will look to disrupt the English set-piece and leverage their passionate home crowd, forcing Borthwick's men to match their physicality from the opening whistle.

sections.heading: England seek to build momentum
sections.body: Following their resounding 73-8 victory over Fiji, England currently sit seventh in the Nations Championship table with one win and one defeat. A positive result in this final round would help Borthwick's side climb the standings and solidify their progress. Conversely, a setback against Los Pumas would undo the confidence gained from their recent triumph at Everton's stadium.

sections.heading: Dupont-led squad refines tactics after Wallabies win
sections.body: Following a high-scoring rebound victory over Australia, captain Antoine Dupont and his newly integrated Toulouse and Montpellier cohorts are driving the intensity in camp. Having missed the opening-round defeat, the late-arriving stars have fully settled into the French setup. The squad is now focused on finding tactical consistency as they prepare for their upcoming clash against Japan.

sections.heading: Calf injury keeps Penaud out of Saturday's Test
sections.body: Winger Damian Penaud remains sidelined and will not feature in Saturday's Test against Japan. He was ruled out of the rest of the July tour after sustaining a calf injury during France's narrow 34-32 tournament-opening defeat in New Zealand. The recovery process continues back home, leaving Les Bleus to finalize their backline options without one of their premier finishers.

sections.heading: Japan seek redemption after defeat against Ireland
sections.body: Japan enter the clash searching for momentum after suffering a tournament defeat against Ireland. While the hosts were convincingly beaten 52-12 during their last meeting against Les Bleus in Paris, they are a side that plays with no fear. The visitors must remain highly disciplined to prevent their opponents from finding rhythm and orchestrating a home-soil upset.

sections.heading: Fourth-place France chase second win to track leaders
sections.body: France currently sit fourth in the tournament standings with one win and one defeat from their opening two fixtures. Bouncing back from their tight 34-32 opening-round loss to New Zealand with a 42-26 victory over Australia, Les Bleus need another dominant performance. A second consecutive win would keep them within striking distance of the frontrunners as the tournament moves forward.

sections.heading: Irish rugby mourns Philip Doyle
sections.body: Ireland prepares for the third round of the tournament following a 36-20 victory over Japan. The Irish rugby community is mourning the death of former women's head coach Philip Doyle, who led the team to a Grand Slam and a win over New Zealand. No team announcement for the upcoming fixture was reported in today's coverage.

sections.heading: Squad depth tested by injuries
sections.body: Ireland entered the Nations Championship with an injury-hit squad according to reports at the start of the tournament. No fresh injury updates were provided in today's coverage following the win over Japan. The camp has not released specific details on player availability as the squad prepares for the upcoming match against New Zealand.

sections.heading: Jordan leads All Blacks attack
sections.body: New Zealand recorded a 47-17 victory over Italy in their most recent Nations Championship fixture in Wellington. Will Jordan scored three tries in that match to become the all-time leading try-scorer for the All Blacks. Scott Robertson's side remains at home for the third round after scoring seven tries against the Italians.

sections.heading: Ireland remains third in the log
sections.body: Ireland goes into the weekend third in the log after winning its first two matches. A victory in the third round would keep the team unbeaten as the tournament moves toward the final stages. Ireland has scored 69 points across the opening rounds and holds a positive points difference of 18.

sections.heading: Cannone suspension looms after Wellington red card
sections.body: After a heavy 47-17 loss to New Zealand in Wellington, the coaching staff must navigate a likely suspension for Niccolo Cannone. The tough second-rower was sent off during the Round 2 defeat, leaving a hole in the tight five. Despite the scoreline, the side showed promise with a good scrum and will rely on an outstanding midfield to challenge Australia.

sections.heading: Back three fitness concerns persist
sections.body: The squad faces specific availability concerns in the back three following the physical encounter in Wellington. While no fresh updates on the team's recovery schedule were provided in today's coverage, the medical staff is managing multiple existing issues across the outside backs. These fitness tests, combined with the expected disciplinary ruling on Cannone, will dictate the selection options for the tour's final fixture.

sections.heading: Schmidt persists with rookie playmakers
sections.body: Joe Schmidt has opted for another rookie playmaker after the Wallabies fell 42-26 to France on Saturday. Australia has lost nine of its past 10 Tests and currently grapples with calf injuries to fly-halves Ben Donaldson and Carter Gordon. Though struggling for bench impact, the Australians have found a bright spot in halfback Ryan Lonergan, who has been a consistent performer across the opening rounds.

sections.heading: Bottom-of-the-log battle in Round 3
sections.body: Italy enters the third round sitting 11th in the 12-team log after two opening defeats. A victory in the upcoming Test would prevent a winless tour and potentially repeat the 26-19 success over the Wallabies in late 2025. With both sides currently without a win in the competition, the result will determine who avoids the bottom of the table as the championship progresses.

sections.heading: Townsend signals significant squad rotation
sections.body: Gregor Townsend has signalled "a lot of changes" for the return to Edinburgh following the 42-28 defeat in Pretoria. After pushing the world champions at altitude, the head coach is urging his squad to keep believing despite the missed opportunities. Having split the opening two rounds against Argentina and South Africa, the coaching staff is expected to refresh the selection to manage the summer load.

sections.heading: Surgery required for unnamed Glasgow lock
sections.body: Scotland face a significant blow in the second row as a Glasgow lock is ruled out of Saturday's Test for several weeks. The forward requires surgery following the encounter with the Springboks. Additionally, the vice-captain remains a doubt and will be assessed this week after failing a Head Injury Assessment. The medical team is focusing on recovery protocols after the arduous trek through the Southern Hemisphere.

sections.heading: Fiji settle into United Kingdom residency
sections.body: Fiji arrive at Murrayfield having based their camp in the United Kingdom to bypass the travel logistics of the Nations Championship. After an opening loss to Wales in Cardiff, the Pacific Islanders are seeking the financial and high-performance benefits of playing in front of large European crowds. They bring a dangerous offloading game that famously tested Scotland during a record 54-17 victory for the hosts in 2018.

sections.heading: Scotland seeks mid-table climb
sections.body: Scotland sits sixth in the standings after two rounds, desperate to level their record back to the winning side of the ledger. A victory at Murrayfield would prevent the mid-table pack from pulling away and keep Townsend’s bigger-picture goals for the world rankings on track. With a points difference of minus five, the squad must rediscover their clinical edge to climb the live table before the final rounds.

sections.heading: Tandy looks for defensive reset in Durban
sections.body: Following a 35-21 loss to Argentina in San Juan, Steve Tandy’s side must address defensive lapses before facing the world champions. While the scrum showed dominance through Rhys Carré and Dillon Lewis, new defence coach Peter Murchie faces a significant challenge after the team missed 32 tackles against the Pumas. James Botham and Jac Morgan remain the form players in the pack.

sections.heading: Recovery focus following San Juan physical
sections.body: Today’s coverage brings no fresh injury concerns from the Welsh camp following the bruising encounter in San Juan. The squad is focusing on non-contact conditioning and ice-bath recovery sessions as they transition to South Africa. While Jac Morgan had a first-half try disallowed on Saturday, he finished the match without reported ill effects, leading the tackle count and providing a clean bill of health.

sections.heading: Springboks prepare to rotate "big guns"
sections.body: South Africa expect to be bolstered by the return of Siya Kolisi and Lood de Jager for the upcoming Test. After rotating in their victory over Scotland, Rassie indicates further changes are likely to increase experience. Springbok flanker Ben-Jason Dixon warned that Wales are "just getting better and better, and they're tough guys to play against" as both sides prepare for Durban.

sections.heading: Wales target log climb in Durban finale
sections.body: Wales go into the weekend sitting eighth in the Nations Championship log after their defeat to Argentina. This fixture marks the conclusion of the tournament’s opening leg, making a result in Durban crucial to avoid sliding further down the table. With a points difference of +1, a win would help Steve Tandy’s side secure a mid-table position before the competition pauses.

sections.heading: Momentum builds for Santiago clash
sections.body: Los Pumas prepare for the visit of England following their 35-21 victory over Wales in Round 2. The squad carries momentum into the clash in Santiago del Estero as they look to build on a balanced opening to the Nations Championship. While the senior side awaits final selection, the national setup is buoyed by the competitive showing of the under-20s in Georgia.

sections.heading: No reported concerns in camp
sections.body: No fresh injury concerns for the Argentina squad have appeared in today’s coverage following the win over Wales. Without reported setbacks, the team’s medical focus remains on standard recovery cycles ahead of the upcoming fixture. The coaching staff looks to have a full complement of the matchday 23 available as they begin their on-field preparations for the English challenge.

sections.heading: England find form before travel
sections.body: England arrive in South America having ended a five-test losing streak with a dominant 73-8 win over Fiji. Steve Borthwick faces pressure to start hat-trick hero Henry Pollock, while Raffi Quirke has been called into the squad as an injury replacement for scrum-half Alex Mitchell. Mitchell was ruled out with a hamstring injury sustained during the final stages of England's recent victory.

sections.heading: Pumas aim for top-half stability
sections.body: Argentina goes into the weekend fifth in the log after securing one win and one loss from their first two matches. A victory would solidify a winning record for the opening rounds and maintain pressure on the tournament leaders. Given the narrow margins in the current table, a strong performance in Santiago del Estero is vital for their mid-championship standing.

sections.heading: Schmidt doubles down on youth
sections.body: Joe Schmidt has resisted the urge to recall veteran playmakers for his final Test in charge, instead backing his rookie options following the 26-42 loss against France. The Wallabies coach is persisting with a youth-first policy, overlooking Japan-based Noah Lolesio. Schmidt indicated that second-tier Japanese rugby is insufficient preparation for the Test arena, leaving rookies like Declan Meredith and Lachlan Shaw to manage the creative duties.

sections.heading: Playmaker crisis persists through calf injuries
sections.body: Australia’s selection options remain hampered by calf injuries to specialist flyhalves Ben Donaldson and Carter Gordon. Both playmakers were ruled out of Saturday's Test earlier in the window, and today’s reports confirm the pair are still struggling with the notoriously difficult recovery process. Beyond the ongoing vacancy at flyhalf, no fresh injury concerns have emerged from the squad’s initial recovery sessions following the physical defeat in Brisbane.

sections.heading: Azzurri eye repeat of November upset
sections.body: Italy arrives following a 47-17 defeat to the All Blacks, a scoreline that belies a competitive first half where they trailed by just four points. The Azzurri have now beaten Australia twice since 2022, including a 26-19 victory in Udine last November. Despite injuries in the back three and a likely suspension for Niccolò Cannone, Italy’s strong scrum and Australian-born winger Monty Ioane remain significant threats.

sections.heading: Wallabies fight to climb from 10th
sections.body: Australia goes into the weekend 10th in the Nations Championship log after failing to secure a point from their opening two fixtures. Following the 26-42 defeat to France, the Wallabies have now lost nine of their past 10 Tests. A victory is essential to avoid a winless July window and to prevent Italy from leapfrogging them in the standings as the tournament's first stage concludes.

sections.heading: Jones sees clear trajectory despite loss
sections.body: Head coach Eddie Jones insists Japan remains on the right path following their 36-20 defeat to Ireland in Newcastle. Jones noted the performance against both Italy and Ireland proved it was "pretty obvious" the side is moving in the correct direction. With one win from two matches, the Brave Blossoms are focusing on tactical consistency as they prepare for France.

sections.heading: Recovery prioritized after Newcastle physical
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage following the physical encounter at McDonald Jones Stadium. The squad’s immediate priority is recuperation after conceding five tries in the 16-point loss to Ireland on Saturday. While the medical staff has not reported new casualties, the short turnaround necessitates a heavy focus on non-contact conditioning and active recovery sessions this week.

sections.heading: France bolstered by returning stars
sections.body: France enters the week on the back of a 42-26 victory over Australia. While winger Damian Penaud has been ruled out of the match with a calf injury, Les Bleus have bolstered their squad with nine players from Toulouse and Montpellier. This group includes captain Antoine Dupont, who missed the opening round but is now available to provide world-class reinforcement.

sections.heading: Mid-table security within reach
sections.body: Japan enters the week ninth in the standings with a balanced record of one win and one loss. A positive result against France would solidify their position in the middle of the 12-team log and keep them ahead of teams like Italy. With a points difference of just plus-one, every score is critical for their overall tournament ranking.

sections.heading: Selection focus shifts to Ireland visit
sections.body: The All Blacks have turned their attention to the upcoming Test against Ireland at Eden Park following their recent victory over Italy. With Will Jordan breaking New Zealand’s all-time try-scoring record in the 47-17 win, momentum is high. Management is expected to confirm the match-day 23 later this week, with the coaching group evaluating their options to maintain intensity throughout the final phase.

sections.heading: Squad recovery underway in Auckland
sections.body: The squad is currently focusing on non-contact conditioning and recovery protocols following the physical demands of the Italy Test. There is no fresh injury news in today’s coverage regarding the availability of the broader training group. Captain Ardie Savea continues to lead the squad’s preparations while lock Scott Barrett remains sidelined as he recovers from a back injury sustained earlier this season.

sections.heading: Ireland remain unbeaten after Japan result
sections.body: Ireland extended their winning streak to six matches with a 36-20 bonus-point victory over Japan in New South Wales. Head coach Andy Farrell rotated his squad significantly for that encounter, handing four players their Test debuts. Despite the experimental lineup, Farrell admitted to feeling a sense of relief after their opposition kept the pressure on until the final minutes of the match in Australia.

sections.heading: New Zealand hunt for third consecutive win
sections.body: New Zealand enters the next round sitting at 2 of 12 on the Nations Championship log. With two wins from two, the team holds a point difference of +32. Securing a victory against Ireland this weekend is essential for the side to maintain its position at the top end of the table as the competition enters its critical mid-point phase.

sections.heading: Kolisi set for return as rotation beckons
sections.body: The Springboks are set to welcome back captain Siya Kolisi and lock Lood de Jager for this weekend’s Test against Wales, according to reports published Monday. Following a win over Scotland, Rassie Erasmus is expected to shuffle his squad again. Potential selection adjustments include moving Canan Moodie to outside centre and providing more minutes to Ethan Hooker, while Jesse Kriel may be rested.

sections.heading: Ongoing recovery for senior Springbok contingent
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage beyond the anticipated return of key personnel. Rassie Erasmus confirmed that Morne van den Berg is nearing full fitness and is nearing a return to the group. The coaching staff continues to monitor the recovery of players returning to the fold while assessing the fitness of those who featured in the recent victory over Scotland.

sections.heading: Gatland faces defensive questions after Argentina loss
sections.body: Wales travel to Durban following a 35-21 defeat to Argentina, leaving head coach Warren Gatland with concerns regarding his side’s defensive structure. Wales missed 32 tackles in their recent outing and currently face a challenging transition period. Despite the loss, their scrum showed promise against the Pumas, with the visiting props and replacements holding their own during a difficult evening in San Juan.

sections.heading: Boks look to maintain perfect record
sections.body: South Africa enters the upcoming weekend atop the Nations Championship log after securing victories in both of their opening fixtures. With 87 points scored and a points difference of +38, the team remains in a strong position. Building on their successful start, the Springboks will aim to finish the first leg of the tournament with an unbeaten record before the All Blacks' tour begins.
